title: On Co-dimension One Stability of the Soliton for the 1D Focusing Cubic Klein-Gordon Equation
speaker: Wilhelm Schlag
abstract: Solitons are particle-like solutions to dispersive evolution equations whose shapes persist as time evolves. In some situations, these solitons appear due to the balance between nonlinear effects and dispersion, in other situations their existence is related to topological properties of the model. Broadly speaking, they form the building blocks for the long-time dynamics of dispersive equations. In this talk I will present forthcoming joint work with J.

proof_stages.label: Variational models and solitary-wave backgrounds
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up one-dimensional classical field equations from a Lagrangian, lists the phi-four, sine-Gordon, and focusing cubic Klein-Gordon potentials, and identifies their stationary kink or homoclinic soliton solutions through phase portraits.

proof_stages.label: Perturbation equations and spectral data
proof_stages.summary: Linearizing around the kink or soliton gives explicit one-dimensional Schrödinger operators whose zero translation modes, internal mode, negative mode, continuous spectrum, and threshold resonances determine the stability problem.

proof_stages.label: Darboux, scattering, and dispersive linear tools
proof_stages.summary: The Pöschl–Teller hierarchy is factorized by Darboux transforms to reduce the linear operator to the free operator, while Jost-solution and resolvent analysis explains threshold resonances, rank-one long-time terms, and the dispersive estimates needed for nonlinear analysis.

proof_stages.label: Orbital stability from energy coercivity
proof_stages.summary: For kinks without negative modes, the conserved energy becomes coercive after modding out the translation zero mode, yielding orbital stability; for the cubic Klein-Gordon soliton the negative eigenvalue requires a codimension-one center-stable constraint.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic-stability obstructions and Fermi golden rule
proof_stages.summary: Known phi-four results and sine-Gordon obstructions show that internal modes, nonlinear coupling to the continuum, and threshold resonances are the central barriers to proving local or global asymptotic stability.

proof_stages.label: Sine-Gordon model problem and resonant cancellation
proof_stages.summary: After recalling inverse-scattering soliton resolution for sine-Gordon, the speaker explains a nonintegrable-method small-data theorem whose key mechanism is a distorted-Fourier vanishing at the resonant frequencies that removes a quadratic small divisor and leaves logarithmic phase corrections.

proof_stages.label: Codimension-one long-time stability for cubic Klein-Gordon
proof_stages.summary: For even perturbations of the cubic Klein-Gordon soliton lying on a center-stable manifold, the new result gives long-time asymptotic control and an L-infinity decay bound up to an exponential-in-epsilon time scale, using microlocal control near resonant frequencies because the sine-Gordon cancellation is absent.

examples.name: Physical pendulum potential
examples.note: The sine-Gordon potential is identified with the potential energy of a physical pendulum, making the model and its phase portrait more concrete without being used in the proof.
examples.engagement_reason: he identifies the sine-Gordon potential with the physical pendulum and then traces its phase portrait with heteroclinic orbits and explicit kink formulas

examples.name: Sine-Gordon wobbling kink
examples.note: The periodic wobbling kink is used as a concrete counterexample showing why the naive asymptotic-stability statement cannot hold for the completely integrable sine-Gordon equation.
examples.engagement_reason: he uses the wobbling kink’s periodic localized motion to reason that sine-Gordon asymptotic stability in that sense is false

examples.name: Free wave dispersion versus Klein-Gordon dispersion
examples.note: The straight-line dispersion relation for the one-dimensional wave equation is contrasted with the curved Klein-Gordon hyperbola to explain why the mass term yields dispersive decay.
examples.engagement_reason: he compares the free wave and Klein-Gordon dispersion relations, checking curvature/no-curvature and the resulting decay behavior

passes.reason: The free wave dispersion is the massless zero-curvature boundary case used to show dispersive decay fails without the Klein-Gordon mass term.
